title: Anti-smog Educational Garden
designer: Kraków City Council in cooperation with local environmental experts and educators
promoter: Kraków City Council
description: The Anti-smog Educational Garden is a pilot green infrastructure project located in a school courtyard in Kraków. It combines nature-based elements—smog-absorbing plants, greenhouse, sensory path, compost area, and insect hotels—with an educational program for students. Created to fight high urban air pollution, it promotes health, ecological awareness, and community stewardship through direct interaction with natural systems.
physical_innovation: The project replaces asphalt surfaces with permeable soil and planting zones that use specific species to absorb air pollutants. It integrates a greenhouse, gazebo, benches, rainwater-fed irrigation, and a compost area, creating a multifunctional ecological micro-environment in a dense school courtyard.
social_innovation: The garden is managed by teachers, students, and parents through guided learning activities, composting workshops, and collaborative planting events. It empowers the school community to take collective responsibility for air quality and fosters a culture of environmental care and urban gardening in children from an early age.
digital_innovation: No evidence of embedded digital or smart systems.

title: Bosco Verticale (Vertical Forest)
designer: Stefano Boeri Architetti, in collaboration with Arup (engineering) and agronomist Laura Gatti
promoter: Hines Italia, COIMA, Comune di Milano
description: Bosco Verticale (“Vertical Forest”) is a pair of residential towers in the Porta Nuova district of Milan designed to integrate over 20,000 plants, including 800 trees, onto the façades and terraces of high-rise buildings. It combines housing with large-scale urban forestry, acting as a vertical ecosystem that improves air quality, mitigates heat island effects, and promotes biodiversity. The project redefines the relationship between nature and density in high-rise design.
physical_innovation: The project demonstrates how vertical surfaces in dense cities can serve as active ecological systems. The integration of vegetation across the façades and on the rooftops allows for the transformation of building envelopes into living systems that contribute to the urban microclimate and landscape.
social_innovation: Bosco Verticale enhances the liveability of high-rise residential buildings by offering residents access to private outdoor green spaces and views of nature. Although not co-designed, the project spurred public discourse on sustainable living and urban quality, influencing policy and design paradigms.
digital_innovation: The building incorporates sensor systems for plant monitoring (humidity, irrigation), but these are not publicly accessible nor participatory. There is no dedicated digital platform for resident engagement or community interaction linked to the vegetation.

title: Catharijnesingel Canal Re-opening
designer: OKRA Landscape Architects
promoter: Municipality of Utrecht
description: The city of Utrecht has restored its historic ring canal as part of a major redevelopment of the station area. The canal was progressively reopened from 2000 to 2020, restoring almost 6 km of waterway and establishing a green-blue linear park along both banks with interconnected pathways, seating areas, planting, and recreational zones. The project re-established ecological and historical continuity, improved flood resilience, enhanced biodiversity, and prioritized pedestrians and cyclists by reducing motor traffic. It won the European Prize for Urban Public Space in 2022 for its exemplary climate-resilient urban intervention.
physical_innovation: The project replaces a major traffic artery with restored water and adjacent greenery. It introduces stepped canal edges, timber piers, floating platforms, seating docks, and rich planting that connect to the historic Zocherpark. It addresses urban heat and flooding by allowing infiltration, adding canopy shade, and reintroducing the natural water body. The motor traffic was rerouted to prioritize active mobility along the canal.
social_innovation: The canal revival was initiated through a 2002 referendum in which residents voted to remove the highway. The redevelopment has transformed it into a vibrant public space for boating, walking, cycling, and community gathering. It fosters ecological and social inclusivity by reconnecting neighbourhoods and creating shared urban amenities, reinforcing civic heritage and local identity.
digital_innovation: No evidence of embedded digital or smart systems.
c5_impacts: Canal restoration: water volume and length

title: CoolCo’s – Cooling Corners & Corridors
designer: CoolCo team: Koho’, Rév8 & Mobilissimus
promoter: EIT Urban Mobility, New European Bauhaus initiative, local municipality
description: CoolCo’s Cooling Corners and Corridors are small modular structures made from wood, fabric and steel to contain various plants and flowers. They are designed to reduce the impact of heat waves in urban areas, particularly densely populated neighbourhoods with low heat resilience. Their shape and size can be adjusted to fit strategic locations, sidewalks or walls for instance, where they provide refreshing “islands” of greenery to shelter inhabitants from the heat and improve air quality.
physical_innovation: A set of modular, scalable, and relocatable cooling units designed for rapid deployment in heat-vulnerable urban areas—especially in densely built, low-income neighborhoods. Modular wooden‑steel units combine shading fabrics, plantings, seating, and mist sprayers.Thermal tests during the pilot phase showed a surface temperature drop of 7–19 °C, improving comfort during heatwaves.. The units are anchored with minimal intervention, respecting existing pavements and avoiding underground utility disruption
social_innovation: The project uses a Living Lab co‑design methodology: workshops with elderly citizens, parents of infants, low-income households to place and configure units according to their needs. It empowers vulnerable groups to claim public space, encourages social interaction, and increases awareness of heat resilience with community agency
digital_innovation: Thermal imaging and structured surveys were used during evaluation to collect data on micro‑climate and user perceptions.

title: Corredores Verdes (Green Corridors)
designer: No data available
promoter: Municipio de Medellín – Secretaría de Medio Ambiente
description: Corredores Verdes is a large-scale urban reforestation programme that transformed 30 key road corridors and water channels in Medellín into ecological and shaded greenways. The project added over 8,000 trees and 350,000 shrubs, aimed at mitigating the urban heat island effect, improving air quality, enhancing biodiversity, and supporting inclusive mobility. It reclaims public infrastructure—roads, sidewalks, and canals—as green spaces, linking parks, hills, and ecological reserves through continuous vegetated corridors.
physical_innovation: The intervention reconfigures major vehicular infrastructures by narrowing roadways, adding vegetated medians, green embankments, and planted sidewalks. These spaces not only enhance thermal and environmental comfort but serve as structural landscape elements for ecological connectivity across the dense urban fabric.
social_innovation: The project integrated social outreach and inclusion by employing formerly incarcerated people and members of vulnerable communities in planting and maintenance tasks. Public education campaigns and community workshops fostered environmental awareness and shared responsibility for the corridors.
digital_innovation: Although low-tech in its execution, the project applied advanced planning tools including GIS-based climate analysis, ecological connectivity mapping, and microclimate simulation to identify priority zones for intervention and measure environmental impact.
c5_impacts: Reduction of average temperature (air and surface)

title: Corredor Verde Passeig de Sant Joan
designer: Ajuntament de Barcelona – Urban Ecology Agency, BIMSA, Studio JPAM
promoter: Ajuntament de Barcelona – Urban Ecology Department
description: The Green Corridor of Passeig de Sant Joan is a transformation of a key boulevard into a continuous and multifunctional green urban axis. The intervention reduced vehicle lanes, introduced tree-lined paths, permeable paving, native vegetation, and integrated social spaces. It reinforces ecological connectivity and improves pedestrian comfort and biodiversity in one of Barcelona’s densest areas. It is a model of integrated mobility, greenery, and urban habitability.
physical_innovation: The project physically reorganizes the street section, replacing asphalt with permeable materials, installing two rows of trees, vegetated strips, benches, and native plant beds. It enhances thermal comfort, creates shaded areas, and manages rainwater through soil infiltration. The boulevard becomes a living ecological corridor embedded in the mobility infrastructure.
social_innovation: It supports inclusive public use by creating multifunctional, universally accessible spaces. The project promotes community life through outdoor seating, children's play areas, and shaded social spaces. It also rebalances public space in favor of pedestrians and cyclists, improving urban well-being and quality of life.
digital_innovation: No evidence of embedded digital or smart systems.

title: Corte Don Bosco
designer: G124 Renzo Piano
promoter: Bari Municipality
description: An urban regeneration project completed in the San Paolo neighborhood of Bari as part of the G124 program. It serves as a replicable paradigm, a model for regenerating other Italian urban suburbs through strategies of proximity, ecology, and inclusion. The project transformed an anonymous urban void - lacking name, identity, or function - into a recognizable, accessible, and vibrant collective space capable of fostering new social and environmental relationships. The intervention balances low-cost execution with high environmental and social value, and is designed to be replicable in similar underused urban settings.
physical_innovation: The project transforms a previously sealed, heat-intensive urban void into a permeable, climate-adaptive micro-park. The existing impermeable concrete paving was removed and replaced with a 70% permeable surface made of natural binders, inert materials, and quarry stones. 127 young trees were planted during the execution phase; as they mature, they are intended to form a green canopy “roof”, improving thermal comfort through shade and evapotranspiration. At the heart of the intervention is a 24-meter-wide circular clearing (radura), equipped with four circular benches, serving as a social core for collective use and community gatherings.
social_innovation: Through a participatory co-design process, the project connected the technical perspectives of the designers with those of the residents, reinterpreting the natural forms that cross the neighborhood, restoring meaning to a forgotten space. The project emphasizes community engagement from inception, involving residents through meetings and a neighborhood festival. This builds trust and collective ownership. The design promotes local pride, supports public programming, and aims to strengthen social cohesion in a peripheral district lacking identity.
digital_innovation: No evidence of embedded digital or smart systems.
c5_impacts: Increase in number of trees planted

title: Gardens in the Air (Jardines en el Aire)
designer: Nomad Garden in collaboration with local youth, artists, schools, and cultural facilitators
promoter: ICAS (Seville), Ecosistema 41, Nomad Garden, AES Candelaria, with COAS & S+T+ARTS
description: Gardens in the Air is a pilot circular intervention in the Tres Barrios‑Amate neighborhood of Seville (2020–2021). It transforms residential façades by capturing air‑con condensation to feed vertical gardens and uses native aromatic plants to produce community perfume. Co‑design with local youth and public performances activated public space. It won the NEB Prize 2021 under “Buildings renovated in a spirit of circularity”
physical_innovation: The project repurposes discarded AC condensate water to sustain vertical green installations on façade frames. It reclaims building surfaces to create micro-ecological systems using local plantings and perforated containers. The design converts urban infrastructure into living walls contributing visual, ecological, and microclimate transformation

title: Green Noise Barrier – Urban GreenUP
designer: Valladolid City Council, CARTIF, University of Valladolid, and landscape consultants
promoter: Urban GreenUP (EU Horizon 2020), Municipality of Valladolid
description: Green Noise Barrier is part of the Urban GreenUP project, a European Horizon 2020 initiative focused on implementing Nature-Based Solutions in cities to mitigate climate change effects, improve air quality, and enhance urban livability. The Green Noise Barrier is a vertical vegetated infrastructure designed to reduce traffic noise and improve environmental quality along a major urban road in Valladolid. Made of modular green panels planted with native species, the barrier acts as a buffer between traffic and adjacent residential zones, improving air quality and aesthetics while providing habitat for urban biodiversity.
physical_innovation: The intervention redefines the roadside edge by installing a continuous vertical barrier of modular panels planted with native vegetation. The green wall system includes structural steel frames anchored to the sidewalk, with integrated planter boxes and automatic irrigation. It replaces bare concrete or fencing with a layered, climate-adaptive interface that reduces noise, enhances pedestrian comfort, and improves ecological continuity along the corridor. Below the barrier, soil beds and micro-greening further soften the transition between hard infrastructure and urban nature.
social_innovation: The project increases perceived environmental quality and contributes to social well-being by improving public space comfort near residential areas. While not co-designed, it supports citizen education about NBS through interpretative signage and public awareness campaigns in surrounding neighbourhoods.
digital_innovation: The project integrates digital environmental monitoring (air quality, temperature, acoustic performance) and remote-controlled irrigation systems.

title: Green Shady Structure – Urban GreenUP
designer: Valladolid City Council, CARTIF, University of Valladolid, and landscape consultants
promoter: Urban GreenUP (EU Horizon 2020), Municipality of Valladolid
description: Small-scale, multifunctional green infrastructure developed within the Urban GreenUP project. It combines vegetated pergolas and modular green shelters designed to create shaded, cooler microclimates in public spaces during periods of extreme heat. The structure features climbing plants on trellises, green roof elements, integrated seating, and an irrigation system. It enhances pedestrian comfort, improves the usability of outdoor areas during summer, and raises public awareness about NBS for climate adaptation.

title: Greening of Flagey & Sainte-Croix Squares
designer: Kollektif Landscape + Fallow + Ecorce
promoter: Municipality of Ixelles & Brussels Mobility
description: The project transforms Flagey and Sainte-Croix Squares into climate-resilient public spaces by replacing paved areas with 1,300 m² of vegetation and planting 100 trees. The intervention enhances microclimate, biodiversity, and urban livability while preserving pedestrian flows and cultural uses. It promotes ecological justice and reclaims the square as a shared, green civic space
physical_innovation: The project reclaims hard urban surfaces through ecological design, adding nearly 100 trees and 1,300 m² of greenery to two public squares. It reshapes public space to address climate: replacing paving with layered, permeable soils that reduce heat, manage water, and foster biodiversity. Native, resilient species offer shade and seasonality while preserving flows and urban viewsheds.
social_innovation: The project enhances the vibrant social life of Flagey and Sainte-Croix Square, known for markets, events, and community interactions. Greening supports these dynamics: trees offer shelter and rest, rain gardens become informal gathering spaces, and vegetation softens noise and views. Designed with local input through exhibitions and consultations, the project affirms ecological justice and the right to quality green space in dense urban areas.vegetation softens noise and views.
digital_innovation: No evidence of embedded digital or smart systems.

title: Hammarby Sjöstad eco-urban neighborhood
year: initiated in the late 1990s, completed by 2017
designer: Multiple architecture and planning firms
promoter: City of Stockholm, Stockholm Water Company, Fortum, Stockholm Transport
description: Hammarby Sjöstad is a large-scale eco-district in Stockholm, developed on a former industrial and harbour site. The neighbourhood integrates sustainable mobility, energy production, water and waste systems, and green-blue infrastructure. It is internationally recognised for its innovative “Hammarby Model”, a closed-loop urban metabolism system. Nature-based solutions such as vegetated swales, rain gardens, and waterfront restoration play a key role in managing stormwater and supporting biodiversity, while making public space livable and climate-resilient.
physical_innovation: The district reclaims and transforms a post-industrial waterfront area into a mixed-use, high-density neighbourhood with green courtyards, stormwater canals, bioswales and green roofs. The public realm is designed to integrate ecological processes directly into the urban layout, including open stormwater management as visible landscape elements.
social_innovation: The project embeds environmental awareness into everyday life through educational signage, civic communication, and the Hammarby Environmental Centre. It promotes a culture of sustainability and encourages residents to reduce their ecological footprint through infrastructural and behavioural choices.
digital_innovation: The district’s incorporates a digital environmental information system to track flows of energy, water, and waste. Although not citizen-facing in real time, the system is used for planning, public education (e.g. Hammarby Environment House exhibitions), and urban monitoring.

title: Invasión Verde (Green Invasion)
designer: Vivo Arquitectura + interdisciplinary collaborators
promoter: Municipality of Lima – Gran Semana de Lima 2010
description: Invasión Verde is a temporary pop-up intervention staged in Encarnación Passage, a pedestrian corridor in Lima’s historic center. It transformed an over-paved public space into a playful, ephemeral green oasis using vegetated mounds, drought-resistant plants, and repurposed materials. The installation called attention to the scarcity of green infrastructure in the city and invited people to reimagine their relationship to urban space through direct, embodied experience.
physical_innovation: The intervention used mobile modules made from turf-covered mounds, xerophytic plants in repurposed tires, and seating crafted from recycled construction waste. All elements were non-invasive and designed for full reversibility, allowing temporary transformation of hard surfaces into green, inhabitable public space without altering urban infrastructure
social_innovation: The project was embedded in a public cultural event It fostered awareness, inclusion, and debate about ecological scarcity and public spatial rights in a dense, car-dominated environment.
digital_innovation: No evidence of embedded digital or smart systems.

title: Jardin des Joyeux
designer: Wagon‑Landscaping (François Vadepied & Mathieu Gontier)
promoter: *Local municipality and neighborhood associations
description: On a former 1,600 m² concrete parking slab in the heart of Maladrerie garden‑city, Wagon‑Landscaping created Jardin des Joyeux, a temporary “rock garden” that reclaimed a once noisy, neglected unused lot. Combining gravel, reclaimed asphalt fragments, and over 150 species drawn from pioneer and alpine habitats, the site was transformed into a hybrid wild garden—dense with spontaneous flora, rugged texture, and botanical diversity. The informal design invited local interaction and slow presence in a gritty, low-maintenance setting.
physical_innovation: The project innovates by turning a hard-surface parking area into a spontaneous hybrid landscape using minimal intervention. By layering loose materials—like gravel, asphalt pieces—and enabling self-seeding, the garden mimics wasteland ecology. Over 150 hardy plant species reclaim the substrate, creating a resilient, self-sustaining green patch. This low-cost, low-input approach demonstrates how degraded urban voids can be reactivated ecologically and experientially without heavy infrastructure.
social_innovation: Although temporary, the garden served as a local ecological and social catalyst. Residents recognized it as a "happy garden" replacing a dull, forgotten space. Its informal, DIY ethos—visible in the choice of materials and species—encouraged public curiosity, community ownership, and quiet interaction. It demonstrated that meaningful urban greening doesn't require formal design or high budgets, fostering a new neighborhood culture around spontaneous urban nature.

title: Les Berges du Rhône
designer: Yves Adrien Sarl Coup d'Éclat, In Situ Paysagistes, JOURDA Architectes
promoter: City Council and Urban Community of Greater Lyon
description: Les Berges du Rhône transformed 10 hectares and a 5 km stretch of former roadways and parking on the riverbank into a vibrant, multifunctional public space. The redesign restored pedestrian and cycling access, added green areas, recreational facilities (playgrounds, skate bowls), waterfront terraces, and water features. It reconnects the historic city centre with the Rhône, promoting leisure, ecology, social life, and flood resilience.
physical_innovation: The project spans ten hectares along over five kilometers of the Rhône’s left bank, with a symmetrical layout: natural landscapes at both ends and an urban core near the Guillotière Bridge. The riverfront features two main levels separated by an inclined stone wall. The upper, adjacent to the city, includes a path dividing vehicular and pedestrian flows; the lower, largely pedestrian, comprises woodlands, grassy areas, and terraces, with continuous paths for walking, skating, and cycling, part of the Léman–Mer route. Ten vertical “panoramic points” connect the levels, integrating elevators, stairs, restrooms, fountains, info points, kiosks, and nurseries.
social_innovation: A multi-year participatory process engaged 600 citizens through 15 workshops and exhibitions. The riverfront became a civic stage—hosting performances, sports, relaxation, and community gatherings—fostering a renewed sense of attachment to the Rhône.
digital_innovation: No evidence of embedded digital or smart systems.

title: Les Promenades
designer: Atelier J. Osty (landscape), Encore Heureux (architecture), Les Éclaireurs (lighting)
promoter: City of Reims – Direction des espaces verts, part of Reims Grand Centre project
description: Les Promenades is the renovation of the historic linear park built on 18th-century ramparts in central Reims. It clears invasive parking and reconnects pedestrian flows along a 12-hectare wooded spine. Through a balanced mix of open clearings, understorey plantings, and wooded groves, it transforms the boulevard into a “Central Park à la rémoise”—supporting leisure, culture, heritage, and ecological continuity.
physical_innovation: The design removed paving and parking, restored heritage tree species (chestnut, maple, plane), and introduced stratified planting: meadow-like understorey, clearings, shaded paths, and reflective water bowls on former bowling greens. Seating, platforms, hammocks, and playgrounds were integrated, reshaping spatial experience while reinforcing ecological structure.
social_innovation: The project engaged local communities through workshops and co-design activities. It enhanced social inclusion by providing diverse public amenities—hammocks, play areas, lecture platforms—activating the routines of residents and visitors across different ages, and reestablishing the Promenades as a civic heart of the city.
digital_innovation: No evidence of embedded digital or smart systems.
c5_impacts: Ecological enrichment with thousands of plants

title: Liuyun Xiaoqu District Project
designer: Urbanus Architecture & Design, South China UT (collaboration with researchers and students)
promoter: Local Housing Bureau, Guangzhou Urban Planning Department, community associations
description: The Liuyun Xiaoqu District Project is a community-based retrofitting initiative in an ageing residential compound in central Guangzhou. It aimed to regenerate public spaces, enhance thermal comfort and social cohesion, and integrate nature-based solutions into an established, high-density urban fabric. The project focused on small-scale, resident-oriented interventions such as rain gardens, permeable paving, micro-greening of courtyards and facades, and the improvement of walkability. It serves as a replicable model for sustainable urban upgrading in Chinese megacities.
physical_innovation: The project restructured the existing residential compound without demolition, reclaiming neglected courtyards, narrow alleys, and residual pockets by introducing rain gardens, permeable paving, vegetated ditches, micro-parks, and facade greening. These small-scale yet cumulative interventions improved microclimate, walkability, and spatial quality, creating an adaptive green infrastructure network within a dense and ageing urban context.
social_innovation: The design process included co-design workshops with residents, surveys, and educational events to raise awareness and ensure relevance to the community’s needs. This participatory model fostered a new sense of ownership and responsibility, particularly among older adults and children.
digital_innovation: Academic partners of the project used thermal imaging and digital tools for environmental diagnosis, mapping urban heat islands and permeability deficits. However, the community-facing dimension was mostly in analog form.

title: Passage 56 (Le 56 / Eco‑interstice)
designer: Atelier d’architecture autogérée (aaa) – Doina Petrescu & Constantin Petcou
promoter: DPVI (Paris City planning authority), City of Paris, local associations & residents
description: Passage 56 transformed a disused, sealed passageway behind two buildings into a 200 m² self-managed ecological and cultural space. Through an open participatory process, it introduced a community garden, greenhouse pavilion with green roof and solar panels, composting facilities, rainwater collection, and workshops. The timber structure creates a threshold from street to garden, fostering ecology, collective production, and local gatherings
physical_innovation: The project demonstrates an exemplary case of micro-regeneration through ecological and architectural solutions in an urban interstice. The physical transformation of a formerly sealed, unused passage into a community-managed ecological space is based on low-tech, low-cost interventions: a wooden pavilion with a green roof acts as the entrance and gathering point, supporting both visibility and functionality. Rainwater harvesting, solar panels, compost toilets, and raised garden beds complete a set of infrastructural elements that regenerate the site while enabling the closing of ecological cycles.
social_innovation: Social innovation lies at the heart of Passage 56. The project originated through a bottom-up, open-ended process involving residents, local associations, and activist architects (atelier d’architecture autogérée). The space was not only designed with but by the community. It promotes new modes of urban citizenship, empowering neighbours to become stewards of their environment. This participatory and care-based model of placemaking challenges traditional top-down urban regeneration and foregrounds community agency.
digital_innovation: No evidence of embedded digital or smart systems.
c5_impacts: Participatory regeneration of 200 m² of urban interstice

title: Patrick Blanc’s Green Wall – Musée du Quai Branly
designer: Patrick Blanc (botanist and vertical garden pioneer), with Jean Nouvel (architect)
promoter: City of Paris and Musée du Quai Branly
description: The vertical garden created by Patrick Blanc on the façade of the Musée du Quai Branly is one of the world’s most iconic examples of living architecture. Covering over 800 square meters with more than 15,000 plants from over 150 species, the green wall enhances thermal insulation, biodiversity and urban aesthetics. Integrated into the museum’s external structure, it represents a pioneering blend of architecture, botany, and climate-sensitive urban design.
physical_innovation: The green wall transforms a building envelope into a vegetated surface, creating microclimatic benefits such as thermal insulation, air purification, and sound absorption. Its location along a major urban axis gives the intervention strong visibility and impact on the surrounding urban landscape. Blanc’s hydroponic green wall system is a patented innovation, using a thin metal frame with felt layers that deliver nutrients and water via automated irrigation and fertilisation. It requires minimal substrate and no soil, enabling large-scale, vertical plant growth in dense urban environments.
social_innovation: While not participatory in its development, the project contributes to the collective redefinition of what public-facing institutional buildings can express—blending nature and culture, and raising public awareness of ecological aesthetics and sustainability.
digital_innovation: No evidence of embedded digital or smart systems.

title: Plaça Sant Antoni (Superilla Sant Antoni)
designer: Leku Studio
promoter: Ajuntament de Barcelona (Barcelona City Council), within the Superilla Barcelona program
description: As one of the first physical implementations of the Superilla (Superblock) model in Barcelona, Plaça Sant Antoni transformed a vehicle-dominated intersection into a pedestrian-centric, multifunctional public square. Located in the dense Eixample district, the intervention reclaimed asphalt for community use by introducing permeable paving, trees, seating, play zones, and participatory co-design features. It became a symbol of tactical urbanism and climate adaptation at neighbourhood scale.
physical_innovation: The project removed car lanes and rigid pavements, installing permeable surfaces using panot tiles, tree pits, benches, planters, and play structures. It introduced new shade trees and green infrastructure while enhancing walkability and comfort. The design promoted microclimate improvement, stormwater management and reduced urban heat, supporting the goals of Superilla as a resilient street model.
social_innovation: The intervention originated from community-driven demands and was co-designed with local stakeholders. The planning included public workshops and direct feedback loops. The new square quickly became a social hub for residents of all ages, particularly families and the elderly, enabling inclusive, intergenerational public life.
digital_innovation: No evidence of embedded digital or smart systems.
c5_impacts: Reduction of NO₂ and PM₁₀ emissions

title: Promenade du Paillon
designer: Michel Péna / Péna Paysages
promoter: Métropole Nice Côte d’Azur & City of Nice
description: The Promenade du Paillon is a major urban transformation in the heart of Nice, France, converting a former bus terminal and car park into a continuous 1.2-kilometer green corridor that overlays the buried Paillon River. Designed by Péna Paysages and inaugurated in 2013, the promenade reconnects the dense urban fabric of Nice with the natural geography of the city, providing both ecological restoration and high-quality public space. Stretching from the Théâtre National de Nice to the Promenade des Anglais, the park integrates a rich variety of natural and social functions across its 12 hectares.
physical_innovation: Over 1,600 trees, 6,000 shrubs, and 50,000 perennials create a lush, diverse urban ecosystem with species selected for resilience to the Mediterranean climate and seasonal dynamism. Key features include a large water mirror with 128 programmable fountains, mist sprayers, lawns for play and events, themed playgrounds, shaded paths, and wooded areas. These elements form a landscape choreographed to invite rest, movement, socialization, and climate resilience. It replaces asphalt with permeable soils, cools through vegetation and mist, and brings biodiversity back to the city core.
social_innovation: From its opening, the Promenade became a focal point for recreation, children’s play, public art, and cultural events, bringing together locals and tourists. Its design includes versatile spaces for informal gathering and organized events, fostering intergenerational interaction and urban well-being. Its creation was backed by strategic planning and public support, resulting in a cherished public asset that hosts circa 35,000 daily visitors and has stimulated positive shifts in nearby urban life and real estate.
digital_innovation: The incremental reduction of irrigation based on sensor testing (e.g., tensiometric probes) shows early use of data-driven landscape management.

title: Proyecto MICOS
designer: Pez Arquitectos in collaboration with Madrid Salud and school communities
promoter: Madrid City Council – Madrid Salud, Urban Planning Department, District Councils
description: Proyecto MICOS transforms schoolyards in Madrid into more livable and healthy learning environments. Through participatory workshops with pupils, teachers and families, greenery, shade, permeable surfaces, play equipment and microclimatic improvements were introduced. The project is part of the broader urban strategy "Madrid Ciudad de los Cuidados."
physical_innovation: The interventions include vegetation, shading, partial depaving, and natural drainage systems, turning formerly paved surfaces into active, green, permeable spaces. Furniture and new layouts were implemented through participatory design.
social_innovation: The project is grounded in community participation: children, teachers, families, associations, and technical staff collaborated through co-design processes, enhancing inclusion, gender equity, education, and school-neighborhood relations.
digital_innovation: No evidence of embedded digital or smart systems.
c5_impacts: Improved accessibility and space for child development

title: Re‑Sourcing Commons
designer: Social Design Vienna with Bezirksvorstehung Leopoldstadt and Wiener Stadtgärten
promoter: NEB Champions 2024; local municipal district office (Leopoldstadt), Social Design Vienna
description: Participatory urban regeneration initiative that transformed Vienna’s Fritzi‑Massary‑Park in the Leopoldstadt district into a circular-material public space. Through community co-design workshops, discarded street furniture and materials from city recycling hubs were reclaimed and repurposed to create modular park furnishings—benches, bike repair stands, noticeboards, swings, and smartphone speaker stations. Awarded NEB, it highlights material circularity blended with social innovation in a diverse, high-density public housing area.
physical_innovation: Re‑Sourcing Commons revitalises an underused neighbourhood park through the material reuse of existing municipal infrastructure. By integrating reclaimed benches, bicycle repair points, and modular urban furniture into the park layout, it demonstrates physical innovation in modular design and adaptive reuse. The campus becomes a hub for community repair, social exchange, and spontaneous gatherings.
social_innovation: The co-design process engages local residents—including elderly and youth—through participatory workshops and cultural events. The park becomes a micro-community center fostering stewardship, dialogue, and collective identity. Activating neglected open space as a site for social co-creation promotes inclusion, intergenerational exchange, and neighbourhood resilience.

title: Rijnvliet Edible Neighbourhood (REN)
designer: Felixx Landscape Architects & Planners, De Zwarte Hond, Æ Food Forestry Development
promoter: Municipality of Utrecht, local residents’ foundation “Green Lungs of Rijnvliet”
description: Rijnvliet transforms a 15 ha new residential quarter in Leidsche Rijn into an urban food forest neighborhood. With 1,000 fruit trees and 220 species of edible plants, the public landscape serves multiple functions: ecological habitat, social space, climate-adaptive drainage, and educational setting. Designed collaboratively through co-production with locals and authorities, REN fosters biodiversity, community stewardship, and resilient urban living.
physical_innovation: The project applies food-forest principles in public spaces—multi-layered planting (canopy, shrubs, herbs, climbers, groundcover, tubers), water retention zones, and edible hedgerows. It integrates NbS for heat reduction, rain absorption, and urban biodiversity, all embedded into street layout and play/recreation areas.
social_innovation: A grassroots idea by residents’ group “Green Lungs,” REN was co-designed with the municipality, ensuring community ownership. The edible landscape is used for communal harvesting, education in the school center, and social events like harvest festivals. These practices enhance social cohesion, health, and local food literacy.
digital_innovation: No evidence of embedded digital or smart systems.

title: Rue‑jardin Kléber
designer: Friche & Cheap / Julien Beauque
promoter: *City of Bordeaux, programme PNRQAD “Re‑Centres”
description: Rue Kléber is a historic residential street near Bordeaux’s Saint-Jean station, transformed into a "garden street" through a participatory greening approach. The project replaced impermeable pavement with planted trenches, introduced trees, shrubs, and green walls, and redefined parking zones as shared green spaces. The design incorporated traffic calming measures and fostered local participation via a gardener-mediator, who helped residents co-design, plant, and maintain public green pockets. The intervention aimed to balance ecology, mobility, and social life in a dense urban setting.

title: SET Community Gardens
designer: Co-designed with residents, guided by SET professionals (social/cultural)
promoter: IJplein-Vogelbuurt community network, backed by city, housing groups, and NGOs
description: A citizen-led greening and social activation project in a post-war neighbourhood. It empowers residents to transform underused, degraded urban land into community gardens and multifunctional green spaces. Through co-creation, SET facilitates urban gardening, community dinners, skill-sharing, and inclusive placemaking. The project strengthens neighbourhood ties, promotes intercultural dialogue, and reclaims collective agency over public space, fostering a deep sense of belonging. In 2024, it received the NEB Prize for connecting generations from diverse socio-cultural contexts through permaculture gardens and urban centres.
physical_innovation: The project reclaims concrete yards, residual spaces between housing blocks, and fenced utility plots. These are transformed through raised beds, edible planting, composting, and rainwater collection. Lightweight, modular, and mobile structures allow interventions to adapt to varied site conditions. The physical design is informal yet ecologically functional, favouring permeability, biodiversity, and human comfort.

title: Skanderbeg Square
designer: 51N4E with Anri Sala
promoter: No data available
description: Skanderbeg Square, once a traffic hub, is now a 40,000 m² pedestrian plaza in Tirana. A flat, monumental surface with stones from across Albania symbolizes unity, while water features animate the space. Twelve surrounding gardens form a green belt. The redesign enhances civic identity, ecology, and accessibility, earning the 2018 European Prize for Urban Public Space.
physical_innovation: The square’s physical transformation replaces a chaotic traffic node with a unified, pedestrian-first space. A slight slope toward the centre creates a monumental yet accessible gesture. Natural stones from across the region compose a tessellated mosaic floor. Water mist and fountains offer microclimatic cooling. A surrounding ring of thematic green gardens provides biodiversity, shade, and social microzones.
social_innovation: The design opens a formerly inaccessible and symbolic authoritarian space to the public. Public consultations and cultural stakeholders shaped the functions of the gardens and uses of the central platform. Today, Skanderbeg Square hosts events, markets, protests, festivals, and informal daily use—becoming an open civic commons. The transformation promotes ownership, civic identity, and symbolic inclusivity through design.
digital_innovation: Digital tools were likely used in participatory design, visualization, and public engagement processes by the design team.
c5_impacts: Transformation into a 40,000 m² pedestrian square

title: Tempelhofer Feld (Tempelhof Park)
designer: Nazi-era architects (Ernst Sagebiel) +  RaumlaborBerlin + Urban Catalyst Studio
promoter: Senate of Berlin, Grün Berlin GmbH, and citizen initiatives
description: Tempelhof Park is a remarkable example of adaptive reuse, converting the iconic Berlin Tempelhof Airport’s 386 hectares of runways, terminal, and surrounding land into one of the world’s largest inner-city public parks. After closing in 2008, the site opened in 2010 for unrestricted civic use. In 2014, citizens voted overwhelmingly to permanently preserve its open, undeveloped status. Managed as “public commons,” the park blends heritage architecture, wide-open grassfields, and community-led programs.
physical_innovation: One of the most radical examples of infrastructural reuse in contemporary Europe. The physical transformation of an airport—once a highly regulated, inaccessible space—into a democratic, multifunctional urban park redefines what open space can mean in a dense metropolitan context. Rather than erasing the site's history, the project embraces its vast, monumental scale and preserves features like the runways, taxiways, and terminal structures, adapting them for new, non-prescriptive uses. These surfaces were not replaced but reprogrammed: the runways now host cycling lanes, roller-skating tracks, and community events.
social_innovation: Tempelhofer Feld is not just a park—it is a civic symbol of grassroots urban democracy. After the closure of the airport, rather than proceeding with a top-down redevelopment, Berliners mobilized through citizen initiative, to force a binding referendum in 2014. This outcome entrenched legal protections for the space and inspired the creation of a collaborative governance model, where local groups propose and manage temporary or semi-permanent interventions. The field has become a testing ground for civic imagination—offering not only recreation, but also a platform for social cohesion, cultural expression, and inclusive urban life.
digital_innovation: No evidence of embedded digital or smart systems.

title: Trees for Trees platform
designer: No data available
promoter: Haringey Council and Trees for Cities, Mayor of London, Green Recovery Challenge Found
description: Trees for Streets is a civic crowdfunding and sponsorship platform designed to empower citizens to fund and request the planting of street trees in their neighbourhoods. Launched in collaboration with local councils, the project allows residents to identify locations for new trees, make financial contributions, and support maintenance. This initiative improves tree equity, raises awareness about urban greenery, and promotes shared responsibility for nature in the city.
physical_innovation: The project enhances the physical landscape of multiple boroughs by increasing street tree coverage in underserved or treeless areas. Rather than planting in existing parks, it focuses on interstitial and infrastructural spaces—sidewalks, verges, and pavement pockets—introducing nature directly into the everyday urban fabric.
social_innovation: Trees for Streets' represents a strong model of civic engagement and participatory governance, enabling residents to become co-promoters of environmental improvements. The platform also includes educational campaigns, neighbourhood outreach, and long-term stewardship partnerships between citizens and municipalities. Empowering residents to directly improve their neighbourhood is an effective way to increase community resilience and helps break down barriers between councils and residents.
digital_innovation: The platform uses a digital interface that allows mapping, sponsorship (online donations), request, and tracking of new trees, integrating GIS and civic tech tools to simplify participation and urban forest planning and automated workflows between citizens and local authorities.

title: Watersquare Benthemplein
designer: De Urbanisten studio
promoter: City of Rotterdam, Schieland–Krimpenerwaard Water Board, Rotterdam Water Authority
description: Water Plaza Benthemplein is a multifunctional public square in Rotterdam designed to function both as recreational space and temporary stormwater retention during heavy rainfall. The square’s shallow basins and water channels collect and store up to 2 million liters of rainwater, reducing overflow into sewers and preventing floods. When dry, the space serves as an amphitheatre, skatepark, event area, and social hub, uniting utility and urban life.
physical_innovation: The plaza integrates stormwater management into urban design through three tiered basins and stainless steel gutters. During rainfall, the design captures runoff, storing it centrally and delaying drainage. The sculpted landscape doubles as seating, playground, and performance amphitheatre, blending flood resilience with everyday public life.

title: Winkelriedplatz
designer: FRL – Franz Reschke Landschaftsarchitektur
promoter: Canton Basel-Stadt (public contract via competition)
description: Winkelriedplatz is the central green square of Basel-Gundeldingen in front of Basel SBB station. It renovates a former parking area into a multifunctional public space under a mature canopy of ~50 linden trees. The design introduces two sculptural cast-concrete platforms that serve as roller rinks, water features, and informal performance stages. The open configuration fosters play, community gatherings, and a welcoming atmosphere, while integrating seamless access and retention of ecological values.
physical_innovation: The design preserves and elevates an existing arboreal framework—high-stemmed linden trees—by overlaying a flexible urban stage that blends pathways, seating, and water-play areas with minimal excavation. The cast-concrete "intarsia" elements reference 1960s forms, serving multifunctional roles (roller rink, stage, water basin) while preserving the “tree hall” spatial quality. The light, open-plan structure supports diverse uses and ensures accessibility and seasonal resilience.
social_innovation: The plaza’s design emphasizes simultaneous, flexible, unprogrammed use: children playing, casual sports, performances, gatherings—all under the same canopy. The various “entries” and networked paths encourage permeability from multiple quarters. A celebratory inauguration with community events reinforced the square’s role as an inclusive social hub. The space was conceived through competition with community-influenced design principles.
digital_innovation: No evidence of embedded digital or smart systems.

title: Temporary Setup Piazza Rossini
designer: University of Bologna – Department of Architecture
promoter: Municipality of Bologna / Foundation for Urban Innovation
description: A temporary installation converted part of a former car-parking area in Piazza Rossini into a raised meadow of over 300 m², complemented by wooden planters with native shrubs and grasses, and two flower-shaped lighting installations, aiming to test new uses of this university-zone public space and anticipate its full pedestrianization
physical_innovation: The intervention introduced a raised green carpet (15 cm above ground) of more than 300 m², with integrated irrigation and native plants, converting a vehicular surface into a permeable vegetated ground-plane. It also incorporated large “Maxxi Poppy” flower-shaped lighting elements to enhance aesthetics and night-time use.
social_innovation: The project emerged from a co-design workshop with students and local stakeholders (via “U-Lab”), activating participation and reclaiming the square for the community and students rather than cars.

title: Sankt Kjelds Plads & Bryggervangen
designer: SLA Architects
promoter: City of Copenhagen – Copenhagen Climate Adaptation Plan
description: First climate-resilient neighborhood, transforming a major traffic junction and its surrounding streets into a lush, multi-functional urban park. The design integrates nature-based stormwater management, biodiversity restoration, and social space creation. The area now functions as a living, cooling, and adaptive landscape that enhances urban life while mitigating climate risks.
physical_innovation: The project depaves large asphalted surfaces and introduces an integrated blue-green infrastructure system capable of collecting, filtering, and storing stormwater. The topography was redesigned to channel water naturally into planted basins and permeable surfaces, increasing infiltration and evapotranspiration. Over 600 trees and 30,000 plants were introduced, enabling the system to retain and delay up to 24,000 m³ of stormwater during cloudbursts.
social_innovation: Public participation guided the redesign of the area, prioritizing spaces for community use, accessibility, and safety. The square and streets were reimagined as inclusive environments that encourage social interaction, play, and well-being. The transformation also changed local perceptions of green infrastructure—from technical installations to everyday public spaces that improve quality of life.
digital_innovation: No evidence of embedded digital or smart systems.

title: Hamamyolu Urban Deck
designer: Yazgan Landscape Architecture
promoter: Odunpazarı Municipality
description: Spanning 26,088 m² in the former centre of Eskişehir, the intervention reinvents the pedestrian axis of Hamamyolu Street, reconnecting the city’s north and south, creating a continuous pedestrian route of 1.2 km embedded with a glass-and-LED line (22,000 local handmade glass pieces), planting 50 new lindens plus 3,877 ivies and 2,622 shrubs to form a green belt, and transforming the street into a vibrant public space that supports both tourism and daily life
physical_innovation: The project replaces a vehicle-dominated axis with a layered structure: surfaces are over-decked (‘urban deck’), a pedestrian bridge passes over the former road, a continuous LED and handmade glass inlay marks the path, existing and new trees create a green corridor, and integrated play and water elements are embedded into the deck, thereby merging infrastructure, culture, green and blue systems
social_innovation: By reconnecting north and south parts of the city via a pedestrian axis and creating a highly useable public zone with cafés, amphitheatres, playgrounds and seating (free seating capacity increased from 1,000 to 5,000) the project transformed the space into a social magnet that supports cultural identity, craft economy (glass production), community gathering and inter-age usage.
digital_innovation: No evidence of embedded digital or smart systems.

title: Ballerup Boulevard
designer: Marianne Levinsen Landskab
promoter: Municipality of Ballerup
description: The boulevard was transformed from a four-lane high-traffic road into a two-lane green boulevard. The former road surface was narrowed and repurposed into green planting beds, tree avenues, storm-water infiltration areas and wide pedestrian/cycle paths, thus converting a barrier into a connective, human-scale green corridor.
physical_innovation: The project physically retreated the vehicular footprint by reducing from four to two lanes, and established extensive planting beds, tree groves and storm-water infiltration strips along the sidewalks and cycle tracks.
social_innovation: By shifting the emphasis from cars to people and green space, the boulevard now offers wide pedestrian and cycle zones, informal recreation areas, and a more favourable environment for social interaction and neighbourhood connectivity.
digital_innovation: No evidence of embedded digital or smart systems.

title: Little C
designer: CULD (Juurlink + Geluk, in collaboration with INBO Architects and Office Winhov)
promoter: Municipality of Rotterdam + ERA Contour
description: Little C transforms a neglected infrastructural void between highways and canals near Coolhaven into a vibrant mixed-use neighbourhood of 15 compact blocks. Combining brick architecture, pedestrian bridges, lush courtyards, green façades, and roof gardens, the project reconnects fragmented urban fabrics, introduces sustainable water management, and creates new public and semi-public spaces.
physical_innovation: The project overlays dense urban form with multilayered green infrastructure: green roofs, vertical planting, water-retention decks beneath pavements, and new micro-courtyards create a resilient “city within the city.” It merges architectural density with permeability and ecosystem functions.
social_innovation: Little C fosters social connection through shared gardens, semi-public courtyards, pedestrian bridges, and communal roof terraces. The design enables encounters and inclusion within a high-density context, redefining liveability in compact urban quarters.
digital_innovation: No evidence of embedded digital or smart systems.
sources: Juurlink + Geluk / CULD. (2021). Little C by Juurlink + Geluk / CULD Rotterdam. Retrieved from https://landezine.com/little-c-by-juurlink-geluk-culd-rotterdam/

title: Girondins Development Area (ZAC des Girondins)
designer: BASE Landscape Architecture, with EGIS VRD, Transitec, Les Éclaireurs
promoter: SERL – Société d’Équipement du Rhône et de Lyon
description: The area is a 17.5 ha urban regeneration project in Lyon’s Gerland district. The masterplan transforms an industrial site into a mixed-use neighbourhood structured by 2 axes: the Allée Fontenay (N-S green boulevard) and Rue des Girondins (E-W avenue). The design integrates green-blue infrastructure, sustainable drainage systems (SUDS), and inclusive public spaces, turning the site into a climate-resilient, vibrant quarter. The project demonstrates systemic integration of urban morphology, ecological infrastructure, and climate adaptation. The “green and blue backbone” operates as a multifunctional spine—both a hydrological system and a bioclimatic corridor.
physical_innovation: The project transforms a former industrial brownfield into a porous and climate-responsive urban fabric. A continuous green-blue infrastructure — including rain gardens, vegetated swales, permeable paving, and tree-lined boulevards — manages stormwater, reduces heat, and enhances biodiversity. The Allée Fontenay acts as a linear climatic corridor, integrating mobility, shade, and ecological continuity within the dense urban grid. Landscape and infrastructure merge into a multifunctional system that turns the public realm into an adaptive device for climate resilience.
social_innovation: The masterplan promotes social inclusion and everyday interaction through accessible, shaded public spaces and shared courtyards. The layout encourages proximity, community life, and mixed uses along green pedestrian routes. Co-design activities and public consultations coordinated by SERL involved citizens and local associations in defining uses and management, strengthening civic ownership and social cohesion.
digital_innovation: No evidence of embedded digital or smart systems.

title: Freiburger Bächle
year: Origin 12th–13th century; restored and enhanced over time (most recent 19th–21st century)
designer: Historical system
promoter: City of Freiburg
description: The Bächle are narrow open-water channels running along the streets of Freiburg's old town. Fed by the Dreisam River, they form a continuous blue infrastructure originally used for irrigation, firefighting and drainage. Today they serve as a cultural symbol, micro-climatic cooling system and playful urban amenity, fostering social interaction and comfort in the historic centre.
physical_innovation: Reactivation of an historical open-water network as urban cooling infrastructure. The Bächle channel fresh river water through the city centre, improving microclimate, reducing urban heat and enhancing sensory quality. Their continuous, low-depth design integrates seamlessly with narrow medieval streets.
social_innovation: The Bächle act as informal social infrastructure: children play with water, residents and tourists interact along the canals, and the channels strengthen place identity. Their accessibility and symbolic value support community life and cultural heritage.
digital_innovation: No evidence of embedded digital or smart systems.

title: Calle María Díaz de Haro – Climate-Adapted Green Corridor
designer: Bilbao City / Municipal Urban Planning Department
promoter: City of Bilbao (Ayuntamiento de Bilbao)
description: Transformation of a 700 m traffic-dominated street into a green and permeable pedestrian-oriented corridor. The project reduces car lanes, removes parking, widens sidewalks, adds trees and planting strips, and integrates permeable surfaces to mitigate heat and manage stormwater. It creates a distributed climatic refuge and improves ecological continuity between Casilda Park and Ametzola.
physical_innovation: Conversion of a three-lane road into a permeable, shaded green corridor. Two traffic lanes and parking are replaced by wide sidewalks, planting beds, and continuous tree rows. Permeable pavements and vegetated strips control runoff and mitigate urban heat. The works redesigned over 4,500 m² of pedestrian space with permeable surfaces, improved infiltration, and lowered surface temperatures. A continuous corridor of deciduous, perennial, native species enhances shade, evapotranspiration cooling, and ecological continuity. The intervention also added 1,300 m² of retention areas and removed more than 2,800 m² of asphalt.
social_innovation: The corridor becomes a comfortable public space supporting walkability, rest areas, social interaction, and everyday outdoor life. It strengthens pedestrian connections between key neighbourhood facilities and parks.
digital_innovation: No evidence of embedded digital or smart systems.

title: Zollhallen Plaza
designer: Henning Larsen Studio (Landscape Architecture)
promoter: City of Freiburg
description: A 5,600 m² post-industrial plaza adjacent to the historic customs hall, redesigned with water-sensitive urban design. The plaza is disconnected from the conventional sewer system, using infiltration planters and subsurface gravel trenches with filter media to reduce hydraulic load. It incorporates indented surface zones designed as temporary flood zones and recharges groundwater rather than draining rainwater to the sewer.
physical_innovation: The design establishes a plaza that performs as a multifunctional, water-resilient platform. Rainwater is managed on-site via permeable pavers, infiltration planters, gravel trenches and dedicated flood-zones – eliminating direct connection to the sewer and recharging groundwater. The use of recycled hardscape material from the old railyard emphasises resource reuse and harmonises with the historic industrial context.
social_innovation: The transformation turns a former industrial yard into a public square that supports everyday use, social gathering and environmental awareness. By embedding visible water-management infrastructure (planters, flood zones) into a comfortable urban plaza with shade trees (e.g., cherry trees) and seating, the project fosters a public realm that educates, activates and connects citizens with site-specific ecological processes.
digital_innovation: No evidence of embedded digital or smart systems.
c5_impacts: Visible water-sensitive design as public awareness tool

title: Brantstraße – Animal-Aided Design
designer: Landscape architect Thomas E. Hauck & ecologist Wolfgang Weisser (AAD method)
promoter: Bavarian State Ministry for the Environment and Consumer Protection; Munich Housing D.nt
description: The Brantstraße project in Munich is the world’s first built example of Animal-Aided Design (AAD). It integrates habitat features for selected target species into the design of residential outdoor spaces. Nesting structures, feeding habitats, micro-wetlands, flowering meadows and species-specific landscape elements were implemented to enable coexistence between humans and urban fauna. The project transforms an ordinary housing area into a biodiversity-supporting environment embedded within daily life.
physical_innovation: Integration of species-specific micro-habitats into residential outdoor spaces, including nesting boxes, structured vegetation layers, continuous flowering meadows and micro-water elements. The design ensures essential life-cycle requirements (breeding, feeding, shelter) for target species directly within the built environment.
social_innovation: The project raises ecological awareness among residents by embedding fauna-supportive features in everyday spaces. Educational signage and community involvement help residents understand how their environment coexists with birds, insects and hedgehogs, strengthening environmental stewardship.
digital_innovation: Digital ecological tools and AAD planning software were used to map life-cycle needs of animals, matching each need with a corresponding landscape or architectural intervention.

title: Kettingplein
designer: De Urbanisten together with OMGEVING
promoter: City of Ghent
description: The Kettingplein project transforms an under-used small square in the heart of a residential neighbourhood in Ghent into a “climate square” with green raingarden features, removal of car-traffic, and redesigned surfaces. Rainwater from adjacent roads and roofs is directed into the new square, stored and infiltrated (approx. 100 m³ capacity) via bowl-shaped raingarden basins and underground storage. The space is designed as a car-free, shaded, green room for the neighbourhood.
physical_innovation: The intervention reduces hard paved surfaces significantly, replaces them with green spaces and gentle depressions to create a raingarden that stores and infiltrates storm-water up to approx. 100 m³. The square is freed from cars and designed as a shared pedestrian/cyclist zone with seating, shade trees and organic forms
social_innovation: The redesign supports neighbourhood life by creating a comfortable and accessible public space where residents can meet and linger. A participatory process included local residents to shape the design, thereby reinforcing ownership and using the space as a “living room” for the communit
digital_innovation: No evidence of embedded digital or smart systems.
c5_impacts: Local infiltration and water buffering capacity

title: Ciudad Dulce
designer: Municipality of Curridabat + interdisciplinary team (ecologists, urban designers)
promoter: Municipality of Curridabat
description: Ciudad Dulce is a city-wide ecological and social regeneration programme that reconceives Curridabat as a multispecies city. Through pollinator corridors, neighbourhood gardens, permeable streets, composting systems and urban food production, it embeds biodiversity into everyday life. The project reframes nature as civic infrastructure and recognises non-human species as urban inhabitants, reshaping the environmental and social fabric of the city.
physical_innovation: The programme delivers a distributed system of ecological micro-interventions—pollinator gardens, tree-lined corridors, micro-wetlands, permeable sidewalks and community orchards—integrated directly into residential streets, schools and public spaces. This granular strategy improves shade, soil permeability, microclimate and habitat diversity throughout the urban fabric.
social_innovation: Ciudad Dulce pioneers a multispecies citizenship model by recognising pollinators and urban fauna as members of the community. Social innovation emerges through shared gardening, community composting, ecological education, neighbourhood stewardship networks and tools that promote collective care. These practices strengthen social cohesion, environmental awareness and everyday participation.
digital_innovation: Digital transformation is anchored in the “Yo Alcalde” civic platform, which allows residents to report environmental issues, map local needs and collaborate on habitat maintenance. This tool facilitates transparent communication between citizens and administration, enhances participatory governance and supports the monitoring of green infrastructure across neighbourhoods.
c5_impacts: Biodiversity integrated into daily urban life

title: Grätzloasen – Neighbourhood Oases
designer: Lokale Agenda 21 Wien + participating citizen groups
promoter: City of Vienna + Lokale Agenda 21 Wien
description: Grätzloasen are small-scale green installations and parklets created by residents, neighbourhood groups and associations in collaboration with the City of Vienna. By converting parking spaces, street edges and residual areas into micro-parks with planting, benches and shading elements, the programme promotes social interaction, local identity, urban greening and heat mitigation. It is a bottom-up system enabling citizens to design, build and maintain small public spaces that improve liveability in dense neighbourhoods.
physical_innovation: The project transforms parking bays, sidewalks and small paved areas into temporary green micro-spaces equipped with planters, mobile seating, permeable surfaces and shading structures. These lightweight, modular installations reduce sealed surfaces, introduce vegetation and provide immediate improvements in comfort and walkability.
social_innovation: Grätzloasen are co-designed and co-built by residents through participatory workshops and community proposals. This model empowers neighbourhood groups, strengthens local ties, promotes collective care practices and fosters active engagement with public space. The programme provides funding, mentoring and tools to support citizen-led placemaking.
digital_innovation: The programme uses online platforms for open calls, application procedures, mapping existing parklets and sharing design guidelines. Digital tools help coordinate community groups, document processes and disseminate replicable solutions for small-scale urban greening.

title: Orto WOW
designer: OrtiAlti landscape architects and social innovation team
promoter: City of Turin and local associations (within ProGiReg / EU Horizon 2020)
description: Orto WOW transforms a former industrial complex in Mirafiori Sud, Turin, into a productive green infrastructure combining a rooftop garden, a pollinator-friendly garden, an apiary and community spaces. The project enhances biodiversity, supports urban food production, and introduces nature as a driver of social inclusion, environmental education and neighbourhood regeneration. Orto WOW is part of the EU project ProGiReg and integrates ecological restoration with community-based management.
physical_innovation: The intervention includes a rooftop garden, raised wooden beds, pollinator-friendly planting, a small apiary, permeable surfaces and micro-habitat structures. These elements reactivate a formerly sealed industrial site, increase biodiversity, create ecological corridors and provide new outdoor spaces for community use.
social_innovation: Orto WOW enables shared governance through a collaboration pact among associations, citizens, cooperatives and municipal actors. Activities include environmental education, collective gardening, workshops, volunteering, social agriculture and inclusion initiatives, reinforcing community cohesion and shared care of the space.
digital_innovation: No evidence of embedded digital or smart systems.
c5_impacts: Ecological regeneration of a post-industrial site

title: Parque Bicentenario
designer: El Equipo Mazzanti
promoter: City of Bogotá
description: Parque Bicentenario is a major urban regeneration project that reconnects two areas of Bogotá long divided by the construction of Avenida El Dorado. Built as a large elevated park spanning over a transport corridor, it integrates terraces, ramps, plazas and green spaces to restore pedestrian continuity and expand public life. The project physically “stitches” an urban wound by recreating links to the historic Parque de la Independencia while offering new areas for leisure, culture, mobility and ecological restoration.
physical_innovation: The park creates a continuous elevated landscape above an existing road trench, combining structural decking, terraced platforms, vegetated slopes, plazas, and pedestrian ramps. It reintroduces green areas over a highly artificial setting and reconnects broken urban edges through walkable infrastructure, integrating vegetation and rest areas into a complex multi-level design.
social_innovation: By bridging neighbourhoods once physically divided, the project improves accessibility to cultural institutions and creates new inclusive public spaces for walking, gathering, leisure and informal activities. The restoration of continuity to Parque de la Independencia revives historical memory and strengthens social attachment to the area, fostering renewed urban identity.
digital_innovation: No evidence of embedded digital or smart systems.

title: Linear Park Ferrocarril de Cuernavaca
designer: Gaeta-Springall Arquitectos
promoter: Government of Mexico City
description: The Linear Park Ferrocarril de Cuernavaca transforms a former railway corridor into a continuous urban greenway, reconnecting neighbourhoods across Mexico City through a 4.5 km landscape strategy. The built first phase (1.4 km) integrates forest-like planting, pedestrian and cycling paths, plazas, sports areas and ecological corridors. It restores public space in dense urban areas, promotes active mobility, and creates a linear urban forest that links 22 neighbourhoods.
physical_innovation: The project converts a disused railway into a multifunctional green corridor with dense tree planting, permeable paths, sports areas, rest zones and ecological micro-habitats. It creates a linear urban forest, integrating nature with mobility, recreation and neighbourhood reconnection.
social_innovation: The park provides safe, inclusive and accessible public space in areas lacking recreational infrastructure. It encourages walking and cycling, supports everyday social life and fosters a shared sense of identity along neighbourhoods historically separated by the railway infrastructure.
digital_innovation: No evidence of embedded digital or smart systems.
c5_impacts: Green reactivation of a disused railway corridor

title: Îlots de fraîcheur (Isles of Coolness)
designer: City of Paris
promoter: City of Paris
description: The Îlots de fraîcheur programme is a city-wide network of cooling spots designed to help Paris cope with extreme heat. The initiative maps and enhances more than 1,400 public cool areas—including shaded parks, fountains, misting devices, permeable plazas, and naturally cooled public buildings—offering accessible refuges during heatwaves. The system creates distributed thermal comfort infrastructure integrated into urban life and the city's heatwave emergency plan (Plan Canicule).
physical_innovation: The programme introduces a distributed system of cooling infrastructures across Paris, combining vegetation, water, permeable materials and purpose-built micro-architectures. In addition to shaded parks and misting devices, several pilot îlots de fraîcheur incorporate lightweight canopy structures, refrigerated benches, and shaded seating modules equipped with evaporative cooling or integrated airflow systems. These micro-architectures create comfortable resting zones even in highly mineral urban areas, extending the cooling effect to streets, squares and schoolyards where natural shade is limited. The combination of vegetated surfaces, permeable pavements, water atomisation and engineered cooling structures forms a hybrid NBS–tech system that reduces local heat accumulation and enhances thermal comfort in dense neighbourhoods.
social_innovation: The programme prioritizes vulnerable groups (derly, children, isolated individuals) by providing a publicly accessible map of cool areas, extended opening hours of public buildings, and community awareness campaigns. The system reshapes heatwave response as a shared civic responsibility.
digital_innovation: A digital interactive map (“Paris – Où se rafraîchir ?”) displays all cooling spots in real time, including opening hours, shade levels, water points and accessibility features. The tool is integrated with the city’s heatwave alert system and mobile applications.

title: Bankside Urban Forest
designer: Witherford Watson Mann Architects + Better Bankside Urban Design Team
promoter: Better Bankside BID and London Borough of Southwark
description: Bankside Urban Forest is a long-term urban regeneration strategy that reimagines the dense and mineral fabric of the Bankside district as a connected network of green streets, pocket parks, planted squares and pedestrian routes. Rather than creating a single forest, the project implements distributed micro-interventions—tree planting, widened sidewalks, seasonal gardens, vegetated benches and shaded civic spaces—to enhance ecological connectivity, thermal comfort, biodiversity and social life. The strategy is co-developed with local businesses, cultural institutions (such as Tate Modern) and residents.
physical_innovation: Transformation of streets and leftover spaces into a distributed system of green infrastructures: tree-lined routes, permeable surfaces, planted benches, vegetated viaducts, pocket parks and seasonal gardens. Streets are redesigned to prioritise pedestrians and create shaded microclimates, improving walkability and environmental resilience.
social_innovation: The programme is co-created with residents, businesses and cultural organisations. It supports community stewardship, activates underused areas, fosters social cohesion and creates opportunities for participation in urban greening. Public spaces become shared neighbourhood assets shaped by local identity.
digital_innovation: No evidence of embedded digital or smart systems.
c5_impacts: Distributed greening of a dense urban district
